facts.text: Shrewsbury was founded in 1665 by Quaker settlers seeking religious freedom and fertile lands in the Navesink region.
The town’s name honors Shrewsbury in Shropshire, England, reflecting the settlers’ desire for a self‑reliant agrarian community.
A research library in Shrewsbury holds over 500 books focused on U.S., New Jersey, Monmouth County, and local history.
The Freehold and Jamesburg Railroad, once a vital link, was abandoned in the early 1930s, marking the end of a key transportation era.
Shrewsbury’s early land purchases from the Lenape were formalized with goods and wampum, allowing peaceful settlement without major conflict.
The town’s historic roots are celebrated in a 2019 exhibition titled “Bruce Springsteen: His Hometown” by the Monmouth County Historical Association.
